title: Chapter 18: The Daylight Blitz
content: The coalition's vanguard emerged from the darkness in a wave of claws and shadow and desperate hunger. Fledglings first — the expendable ones, sent to absorb the initial volley so the Barons behind them could advance unscathed. Count Orsi had trained his army well. He'd learned from every battle fought in the ten thousand years since vampires first organized into armies. He knew that the first wave always died.
What he didn't know was that the first wave was dying in sunlight.

The Thorn Gate had been designed centuries ago as a defensive chokepoint, but its original architects had never imagined a battle fought during the day. The gate faced east, toward the surface tunnels, and at noon — when Kael had timed his assault — the sun angled directly through the passage, flooding the narrow corridor with golden light.

The fledglings hit the sunlight and *dissolved*. They didn't burn slowly or scream or fight against the inevitable. They simply ceased to exist. One moment they were a wave of fangs and fury, the next they were ash drifting on the tunnel wind, their remains coating the stone floor in a fine grey powder.
"Hold formation!" Marcus shouted. "Wait for the Barons!"

The Barons came next. They were smarter than the fledglings — they'd seen what happened to the first wave and adjusted. They came in low, hugging the shadows, using the bodies of their fallen comrades as shields against the light. Some of them had brought physical shields — slabs of stone and iron that they held in front of them as they advanced. It wasn't enough to stop the sunlight completely, but it was enough to slow the burn. A Baron who should have died in seconds might last a minute. A minute was an eternity in combat.
Kael met them at the gate.

He was a Viscount now. Two heart-bloods consumed — a Baron's and a Viscount's — and the power of both flowing through his veins. He moved faster than the Barons could track, his silver blade carving through shield and armor and flesh with equal ease. The first Baron died before he realized Kael was there. The second got his claws up in time to deflect the killing blow, but not fast enough to stop the follow-up strike that took his head. The third tried to run and learned that running from a Viscount was like running from the sun itself.
"PUSH!" Marcus roared, and the Polaris Guard surged forward.

Fifty-three soldiers against hundreds. Daylight-immune vampires against the terrified remnants of an army that had never fought in the light. The coalition's front line shattered. Barons broke and fled. Fledglings burned. The Viscounts who'd been commanding from the rear suddenly found themselves at the front, their soldiers gone, their strategy in ruins, the impossible daywalker coming toward them with a silver blade and a cold smile.

Count Orsi watched from his command tent, deep in the tunnels where the sunlight couldn't reach. His golden eyes were fixed on a scrying mirror — a blood-magic artifact that showed him the battle in real time, the images flickering across its surface like reflections on dark water.

He watched his vanguard die. He watched the Barons break. He watched the daywalker cut through his army like a scythe through wheat, his impossible soldiers behind him, their crossbows singing silver death into the darkness.
"Interesting," Orsi murmured.
"My lord." One of his Viscount commanders — a young vampire named Aldric, newly promoted and eager to prove himself — stepped forward. "The vanguard has collapsed. If we don't reinforce—"

"I know what I'm watching, Aldric." Orsi's voice was calm. Almost bored. "The Viscount Ashworth has done exactly what I expected him to do. He's used his daylight immunity to break our front line at the chokepoint. He's relying on the narrow passage to negate our numerical advantage. Fifty-three soldiers can hold the Gate against an army if the army can only approach ten at a time."
"Then what do we do?"

"We change the battlefield." Orsi raised one hand, and the scrying mirror's image shifted — pulling back, showing the tunnels above and below the Thorn Gate. "Ashworth is defending a single pass. But there are other ways into Greyhaven. Service tunnels. Ventilation shafts. Routes that were sealed centuries ago and forgotten by everyone except the Counts who've been planning this invasion for a hundred years."
Aldric's eyes widened. "You knew he'd hold the Gate."

"Of course I knew. The daywalker is predictable — he always attacks at noon, always uses the sun, always relies on the assumption that his enemies haven't adapted." Orsi's smile was cold. "Send the flanking force. Three Viscounts, thirty Barons, all the fledglings we can spare. They take the service tunnels and emerge behind the Polaris Guard. Ashworth will be pinned between two forces. And without the sun to protect him from the rear..."
"He burns."

"He burns." Orsi settled back into his command chair. "Tell the flanking force they have one hour. After that, I'm going to introduce the daywalker to a Count who's had four centuries to learn every trick the sun can't save him from."
